The **University of Michigan Ross School of Business** (branded as **Michigan Ross**) is the [business school](/wiki/Business_school "Business school") of the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan"), a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Ann Arbor, Michigan](/wiki/Ann_Arbor,_Michigan "Ann Arbor, Michigan").

The school was originally established in 1924 as the School of Business Administration. Today, it offers [bachelor's](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), [master's](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree"), and [doctoral](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") degrees, as well as an [executive education](/wiki/Executive_education "Executive education") program. Michigan Ross also collaborates with other colleges and schools at the University of Michigan to offer [dual degree](/wiki/Dual_degree "Dual degree") programs. Additionally, the school's Executive Education program includes a Distinguished Leader Certificate.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Michigan Ross maintains the tenth largest endowment among all [business schools in the United States](/wiki/List_of_business_schools_in_the_United_States "List of business schools in the United States"), with a total of US$435 million as of 2016.[[1]](#cite_note-:8-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history (1900–1923)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history (1900–1923)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Henry_Carter_Adams.png/250px-Henry_Carter_Adams.png)](/wiki/File:Henry_Carter_Adams.png)

[Henry Carter Adams](/wiki/Henry_Carter_Adams "Henry Carter Adams"), former chairman of the economics department

The first business courses were offered at the [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") in 1900. Economics Department Chairman [Henry Carter Adams](/wiki/Henry_Carter_Adams "Henry Carter Adams") oversaw the expanding practical courses to prepare students for business careers. The idea for the school came from the economics department.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5) In 1918, the university's [College of Literature, Science, and the Arts](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_College_of_Literature,_Science,_and_the_Arts "University of Michigan College of Literature, Science, and the Arts") began issuing a Certificate of Business Administration. In 1923, University President [Marion LeRoy Burton](/wiki/Marion_LeRoy_Burton "Marion LeRoy Burton") hired [Edmund Ezra Day](/wiki/Edmund_Ezra_Day "Edmund Ezra Day") to serve as the founding dean of a new business school.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Founding (1924–1925)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Founding (1924–1925)")]

The **University of Michigan School of Business Administration** was founded in 1924; it offered a two-year [Master of Business Administration](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration") after three years of general studies. There were 14 faculty members, including one of the first women to be part of a business school. In 1925, the Bureau of Business Research was founded to facilitate and coordinate faculty research and publish research monographs and case studies.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Clare Griffin era (1926–1943)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Clare Griffin era (1926–1943)")]

In 1926 after serving three years, Day was replaced as dean by Clare Griffin, who initially came to the university to teach marketing. The same year, faculty member [William Andrew Paton](/wiki/William_Andrew_Paton "William Andrew Paton") founded *[The Accounting Review](/wiki/The_Accounting_Review "The Accounting Review")*. In 1935, the school began offering a PhD in Business Administration. In 1938, the first evening [Master of Business Administration](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration") (MBA) classes were offered. In 1940, school enrollment reached 200 students. Between 1942 and 1943, a [Bachelor of Business Administration](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Business_Administration "Bachelor of Business Administration") (BBA) degree was introduced.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Russell Stevenson era (1943–1959)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Russell Stevenson era (1943–1959)")]

In 1943, Russell Stevenson became the school's third dean. In 1947, enrollment exceeded 1,000 students, reaching 800 BBA students and 400 MBA students by 1949. In 1948, the school opened a new building that cost $2.5 million and had a nine-story tower. The same year, prominent economist [Paul McCracken](/wiki/Paul_McCracken_(economist) "Paul McCracken (economist)") joined the faculty. From 1950 to 1959, the school introduced the Public Utility Executive Program, its first stand-alone, non-degree program for continuing education of business executives.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Floyd Bond era (1960–1978)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Floyd Bond era (1960–1978)")]

In 1960, Floyd Bond became the school's fourth dean. The same year, the school launched its first international joint venture in Taiwan and Bond appointed a committee to better integrate global business into the school's curriculum. In 1966, Bond established the Ad Hoc Committee on Educational Programs in Business Administration for Negroes, which tried to attract more African-American students but the initiative and a number of student strikes and other protests in the following years demanded more minority enrollment. In 1971, the school began a $1.5 million expansion of the physical facilities and assembly hall. By 1971, total enrollment exceeded 1,300, more than three-quarters of whom were graduate students with 370 in the Evening MBA Program. In 1972, the school's first two alumni clubs in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and [California](/wiki/California "California"), held their first meetings. In 1974, Alfred Edwards joined the faculty as a professor of business administration and director of the research division, later becoming known as the school's ambassador for diversity.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Gilbert Whitaker era (1979–1990)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Gilbert Whitaker era (1979–1990)")]

In 1979, Gilbert Whitaker became the school's fifth dean and established an enlarged Office of Development & Alumni Relations. In 1980, total degree-program enrollment reached 2,000, including about 600 BBA students, while the number of faculty members exceeded 100. From 1980 to 1989, Whitaker stablished 17 joint-degree programs with other U-M units. In 1982, he announced a $15 million fundraising campaign for three new buildings, including a library. The campaign raised $17 million, and the buildings opened by 1984. In 1983, the school joined the [Consortium for Graduate Study in Management](/wiki/Consortium_for_Graduate_Study_in_Management "Consortium for Graduate Study in Management") and began offering a [Master of Accounting](/wiki/Master_of_Accounting "Master of Accounting") degree. In 1990, the school received a federal grant to establish a Center for International Business Education.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### B. Joseph White era (1990–2001)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: B. Joseph White era (1990–2001)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/B._Joseph_White_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-B._Joseph_White_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:B._Joseph_White_(cropped).jpg)

[B. Joseph White](/wiki/B._Joseph_White "B. Joseph White")

In 1990, [B. Joseph White](/wiki/B._Joseph_White "B. Joseph White") became the school's sixth dean. In 1991, White and Associate Dean Paul Danos introduced a major MBA curriculum overhaul by initiating the Multidisciplinary Action Project (MAP), a full-time, seven-week project that allowed teams of students to work on real-world business challenges for sponsor companies. After a pilot run, it became part of the MBA core curriculum in 1993. In 1995, an international MAP option was created as a separate program. In 2015, MAP became an option in the undergraduate curriculum.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1992, the [William Davidson Institute](#The_William_Davidson_Institute) was founded. In 1995, the Tauber Institute was founded as a joint project with the [College of Engineering](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_College_of_Engineering "University of Michigan College of Engineering"). In 1996, the Global MBA Program is introduced, allowing students to study abroad in one-month terms. The same year, the Frederick A. and Barbara M. Erb Environmental Management Institute was established. In 1999, the Samuel Zell and Robert H. Lurie Institute for Entrepreneurial Studies was founded. In 2000, Sam Wyly Hall opened to house Executive Education and other programs. The same year, the number of faculty members approached 200. In 2001, the school introduced an Executive MBA Program, which targeted business leaders in supervisory positions.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

During White's tenure, other initiatives, such as research on how to supplement the [GMAT](/wiki/Graduate_Management_Admission_Test "Graduate Management Admission Test"), helped cement the school's reputation for innovations that produce business leaders.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Robert J. Dolan era (2001–2011)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Robert J. Dolan era (2001–2011)")]

In 2001, [Robert J. Dolan](/wiki/Robert_J._Dolan_(educator) "Robert J. Dolan (educator)") became the school's seventh dean. In 2002, the John R. and Georgene M. Tozzi Electronic Business and Finance Center was established. In 2003, several faculty members published "Positive Organizational Scholarship: Foundations of a New Discipline", a study on how to create positive organizations. The school would later found the Center for Positive Organizations. In 2004, alumnus [Stephen M. Ross](/wiki/Stephen_M._Ross "Stephen M. Ross") donated US$100 million to the school, which was renamed the **Stephen M. Ross School of Business**.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5) This was the largest-ever gift to a U.S. business school and to the University of Michigan. The Ross gift funded a campus overhaul; the school demolished 180,000 square feet (17,000 m2) of existing building space and renovated or added 270,000 square feet (25,000 m2).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 2006, the Ross Leadership Initiative, later renamed the Sanger Leadership Center, was established to improve the leadership development of MBA students. In 2009, the school's Master of Supply Chain Management degree program was introduced. The same year, the $145 million Stephen M. Ross building opened. In 2010, a Weekend MBA Program was introduced, allowing students to complete an MBA in two years while working full-time.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Alison Davis-Blake era (2011–2016)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Alison Davis-Blake era (2011–2016)")]

In 2011, [Alison Davis-Blake](/wiki/Alison_Davis-Blake "Alison Davis-Blake") became the school's eighth and first female dean.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 2012, the Executive MBA Program was expanded to a second campus in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 2013, Stephen M. Ross made a second $100 million gift as a part of a larger fundraising effort; the gift aimed "to help complete the vision of a unified business school complex, including one new building, one fully renovated building, and other improvements".[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5) The same year, a Minor in Business is offered for non-business undergraduate students.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)[[11]](#cite_note-11) In late 2013, the school launched a major fundraising campaign.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 2014, a new 10-month Master of Management degree was introduced.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The same year, the school began sponsoring the annual Positive Business Conference.[[14]](#cite_note-14) During Davis-Blake's tenure, the school broadened its global presence, increasing the number of overseas experiences for students and beginning a partnership with [Shanghai Jiao Tong University](/wiki/Shanghai_Jiao_Tong_University "Shanghai Jiao Tong University").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Desai Accelerator for startup businesses—a joint effort between Ross and the [University of Michigan College of Engineering](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_College_of_Engineering "University of Michigan College of Engineering")—opened in downtown [Ann Arbor](/wiki/Ann_Arbor "Ann Arbor") in 2014.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The same year, Davis-Blake oversaw a large, donor-funded construction project[[18]](#cite_note-18) that includes the new Jeff T. Blau Hall,[[19]](#cite_note-19) as well as renovations to several older buildings on the business campus.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 2015, Davis-Blake announced that she would step down at the end of her five-year term in 2016 to pursue a broader role in higher education.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In 2015, Michigan Ross announced the Alumni Advantage program that offers free lifetime open-enrollment executive education tuition for all its degree alumni and half-price tuition for non-Ross UM alumni.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In 2016, the Jeff T. Blau Hall and the renovated Kresge Hall opened.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Scott DeRue era (2016–2021)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Scott DeRue era (2016–2021)")]

In 2016, [Scott DeRue](/wiki/Scott_DeRue "Scott DeRue") became the school's ninth dean. He joined the Michigan Ross faculty in 2007 and has been an associate dean. In 2017, enrollment exceeded 3,000 students, including about 1,600 BBA students and 800 full-time MBA students. Nearly 40% of incoming BBA students are women, and about half are from other states.[[5]](#cite_note-Ross_History-5)

### Francine Lafontaine – Interim (2021–2022)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Francine Lafontaine – Interim (2021–2022)")]

Francine Lafontaine stepped in as the business school's interim dean, effective May 24, 2021. Lafontaine began her academic career as an assistant professor at Carnegie Mellon University. She joined the Michigan Ross faculty as an assistant professor in 1991 and was promoted to associate professor in 1995 and to professor in 2000. She was the Jack D. Sparks – Whirlpool Corporation Research Professor of Business Administration from 2005–07 and has held the William Davidson Professor of Business Administration chair since 2010.

From 2016–20, Lafontaine served as the senior associate dean for faculty and research at Michigan Ross. Prior to that, she served as the director at the Bureau of Economics for the U.S. Federal Trade Commission from fall of 2014 to the end of 2015. She also was the chair of the Business Economics and Public Policy group at Ross from 2003–2012.

Her research focuses on industrial organization, vertical relationships, contracting and franchising, and entrepreneurship, along with related public policy issues. She has received several research grants, including from the Sloan Foundation and the National Science Foundation.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

On May 19, 2022, the University of Michigan announced that it had selected [Sharon F. Matusik](/wiki/Sharon_F._Matusik "Sharon F. Matusik"), the culmination of an eight-month-long national search for the next dean. Matusik's term began on August 1, 2022, and ends on July 31, 2027.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Facilities and institutes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Facilities and institutes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/University_of_Michigan_Central_Campus-Ross_School_of_Business.jpg/330px-University_of_Michigan_Central_Campus-Ross_School_of_Business.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Michigan_Central_Campus-Ross_School_of_Business.jpg)

The Ross School of Business facilities on the university's central campus

Upon its establishment in 1924, the business school was located in Tappan Hall, the oldest extant classroom building on campus. The original 1894 wing was designed by the Detroit firm [Spier & Rohns](/wiki/Spier_%26_Rohns "Spier & Rohns"), and the south wing was designed by Luckenbach / Ziegelman & Partners.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

The school moved to its current site in 1948. Authorized by the Regents in July 1945, the site was in the northern half of a block surrounded by Monroe Street, Tappan Street, Hill Street, and Haven Street. It was purchased at the time construction was begun. Ten private dwellings were demolished for to make way for the construction, which began in August 1946. The business school was the first skyscraper on campus and was designed by architects Lee and Kenneth C. Black, of Lansing, Michigan.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

New York City real estate developer [Stephen M. Ross](/wiki/Stephen_M._Ross "Stephen M. Ross") (BBA '62) gave a gift of $100 million to the business school, the second largest donation ever to a U.S. business school and the largest gift to the university in its history. In recognition, the Board of Regents met in special session on September 9, 2004, to rename the school the Stephen M. Ross School of Business. New York-based architecture firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox "Kohn Pedersen Fox") (KPF) designed the 270,000-square-foot building.[[38]](#cite_note-39) In 2013, Ross announced a second gift to the school of $100 million. The second gift was used for facilities upgrades, including high-tech classrooms, a new career services space, and additional areas for practical research; as well as student scholarships.[[39]](#cite_note-40) KPF designed this second phase, as well, including the new-construction Blau Hall, renovated Kresge Hall, and recladded Wyly Hall.[[40]](#cite_note-41) In September 2017, Ross donated an additional $50 million to the school.

### The William Davidson Institute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The William Davidson Institute")]

The William Davidson Institute (WDI) is a not-for-profit, independent, research and educational institute that was created in 1992 when [Guardian Industries](/wiki/Guardian_Industries "Guardian Industries") made a 20-year financial commitment to establish an institute at the University of Michigan Business School. The institute was named for Guardian Industries' chairman, president and CEO [William Davidson](/wiki/Bill_Davidson_(businessman) "Bill Davidson (businessman)"). WDI supports international activity at the University of Michigan by funding research, hosting visiting scholars, organizing seminars and speaker series, sponsoring summer internships, and creating dynamic and current teaching materials.[[41]](#cite_note-globalens.com-42) Since 2006, more than 1,800 MBA students have participated in more than 450 international projects. WDI's publication initiative houses a collection of international business and social impact teaching materials.[[41]](#cite_note-globalens.com-42) A publishing subsidiary named WDI Publishing was founded in 2007.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

### The Erb Institute for Global Sustainable Enterprise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: The Erb Institute for Global Sustainable Enterprise")]

Created in 1996 by a donation from Frederick A. Erb (BBA '47) and his wife Barbara, the Erb Institute for Global Sustainable Enterprise is a partnership between the Stephen M. Ross School of Business and the [School for Environment and Sustainability](/wiki/University_of_Michigan_School_for_Environment_and_Sustainability "University of Michigan School for Environment and Sustainability") (SEAS) to provide education and opportunities for research into business sustainability. The Erb Institute's Dual-Degree MBA/MS Program, in which students earn a Master of Business Administration from the Ross School and a Master of Science from SEAS in 2 1/2 to 3 years, has received many accolades. In 2013, Michigan Ross (Erb Institute) received the top ranking for MBA programs with a Sustainability specialty ([Bloomberg Business MBA Rankings](https://www.bloomberg.com/bw/articles/2013-01-22/mba-rankings-top-schools-for-sustainability))

### The Joel D. Tauber Institute for Global Operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The Joel D. Tauber Institute for Global Operations")]

Joel D. Tauber donated $5 million in 1995 to establish the Joel D. Tauber Manufacturing Institute, which trains graduate engineering and business students in operations management. The program is run jointly with the University's College of Engineering. In 2007, the institute was renamed the (Joel D.) Tauber Institute for Global Operations to recognize increased interest from service, healthcare, IT, and retail organizations. In 2012 the institute was awarded the first UPS George D. Smith Prize from [INFORMS](/wiki/INFORMS "INFORMS")[[43]](#cite_note-44) for its effective and innovative preparation of students to be practitioners of operations research, management science or analytics.

### The Zell–Lurie Institute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: The Zell–Lurie Institute")]

The Samuel Zell & Robert H. Lurie Institute for Entrepreneurial Studies provides the curriculum, program initiatives, community involvement, and alumni outreach activities that provide resources for graduates of the Ross School of Business. In 1999, a $10 million donation from [Samuel Zell](/wiki/Samuel_Zell "Samuel Zell") and Ann Lurie on behalf of her husband [Robert H. Lurie](/wiki/Robert_H._Lurie "Robert H. Lurie") established the Samuel Zell & Robert H. Lurie Institute for Entrepreneurial Studies at the Business School.

### The Wolverine Venture Fund

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: The Wolverine Venture Fund")]

The Wolverine Venture Fund (WVF) is a multimillion-dollar venture capital fund operated by the Ross School of Business.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The fund was started in 1997 by Karen Bantel.[[45]](#cite_note-businessweek.com-46) The fund invests with the active involvement of MBA students, faculty assistance, and an advisory board composed of professional venture capitalists and entrepreneurs.[[45]](#cite_note-businessweek.com-46) It invests in early-stage, emerging growth companies.[[46]](#cite_note-47) The Fund typically provides US$50,000 to $200,000 in seed and first-stage funding rounds[[45]](#cite_note-businessweek.com-46) with other venture capital funds and [angel investors](/wiki/Angel_investor "Angel investor"). The Wolverine Venture Fund, the oldest such U.S. venture, earned $1 million when [Intralase](/wiki/Intralase "Intralase"), a U-M spin-off technology company, went public.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### The Center For Positive Organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: The Center For Positive Organizations")]

Founded in 2002 as the Center for Positive Organizational Scholarship, the center's mission is "to inspire and enable leaders to build high-performing organizations that bring out the best in people". It researches topics such as positive leadership, meaning and purpose, ethics and virtues, and relationships and culture in an organizational setting. Starting in 2014, it sponsors an annual Positive Business Conference.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

### The Sanger Leadership Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: The Sanger Leadership Center")]

The Sanger Leadership Center at Michigan Ross was named for a gift by Steve and Karen Sanger in 2015,[[49]](#cite_note-50) The center offers leadership development programming for all University of Michigan students. Its signature initiatives open to U-M students include the Leadership Crisis Challenge, Impact Challenge, Story Lab, and Legacy Lab.[[50]](#cite_note-51) Sanger uses leadership development theories and tools developed at Michigan Ross, such as the Michigan Model of Leadership.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ross_School_of_Business&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Student life")]

Ross School of Business students publish a newspaper called the *[Monroe Street Journal](/wiki/Monroe_Street_Journal "Monroe Street Journal")* and houses the *Michigan Journal of Business*, the first undergraduate-level academic journal in the field of business.

A Ross MBA community tradition is the Ross MBA college football tailgate, which takes place before and after every home game at "The Bus", a 1985 Ford school bus that is painted in maize and blue, Michigan's colors, and is decorated with a dance floor, a DJ on the roof, and couches in the interior. On game days, The Bus is parked in a nearby Lumber Yard for an event called the Ross MBA tailgate, which attracts over 500 students, alumni, and friends. As of 2013, this tradition had been intact for the last decade.[[52]](#cite_note-53) After the bus "died" in 2013, a group of MBA students started a crowdfunding campaign on [Tilt.com](/wiki/Tilt.com "Tilt.com") to purchase a new mobile tailgate bus.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

*Michigan Journal of Business* is a biannual, [peer-reviewed](/wiki/Peer_review "Peer review"), [open access](/wiki/Open_access "Open access"), [academic journal](/wiki/Academic_journal "Academic journal") that is published by undergraduate students at the [Stephen M. Ross School of Business](/wiki/Stephen_M._Ross_School_of_Business "Stephen M. Ross School of Business") ([University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan")). It was established in 2007 by [William Moon](/wiki/William_Moon "William Moon") and publishes theses, empirical research, case studies, and theories relating to accounting, economics, finance, marketing, management, operations management, information systems, business law, [corporate ethics](/wiki/Business_ethics "Business ethics"), and public policy. The editorial staff is composed of students of the University of Michigan. The journal is abstracted and indexed in [EBSCO's](/wiki/EBSCO_Publishing "EBSCO Publishing") Business Source Complete database.[[54]](#cite_note-55) Archival issues are available at the MJB's website.[[55]](#cite_note-56)
